evidence_quotes.quote: Indeed, several video game companies, including Ubisoft, destroy the games we own.
From one day to the next, the game that we had to pay, and therefore which belongs to us, can be destroyed by the companies that set up its games for our consumers, for unjustified reasons.
This act is unjustified because not only do we lose the game, but we do not receive any reimbursement from their companies that destroy little of our games.

paragraphs.text: Dear Sir/Madam of the European Commission and the Digital Fairness Act. I want to share with you a problem that is beginning to have a massive impact on the world of video games, and therefore has a negative impact on all players around the world. Indeed, several video game companies, including Ubisoft, destroy the games we own. From one day to the next, the game that we had to pay, and therefore which belongs to us, can be destroyed by the companies that set up its games for our consumers, for unjustified reasons. Recently, the ‘The Crew’ game created by Ubisoft in 2014 has just disappeared from online and physical video games stores. The game for those they bought online, just lost the game, never happened, and those who had bought the game in physics were forced by Ubisoft to destroy the game, as they were closed down all servers and any possibility to buy the game again. This act is unjustified because not only do we lose the game, but we do not receive any reimbursement from their companies that destroy little of our games. This is not the only example of a game that can no longer be acquired, Age of Empires Castle Seige, AirMech Arena and still missing and impossible to play again. Several games are likely to disappear, such as Albion Online and Anthem de at EA. The Stopkillinggames Wiki site has more than 358 destroyed, irrecoverable and unplayable games today and hundreds of others with a risk of disappearing one day. We as players feel threatened by these companies, as we did not feel that we had the games we bought, which is unfair and dishonest. For several months, the European Stop Killing Games initiative received more than 1.4 million signatures to prevent video game companies from continuing to destroy our video games. My message is a call that, if possible, add a law to protect our video games that we had bought and which could disappear. I would like to thank you very much for your attention, Madam, Member of the European Commission and the Digital Fairness Act.
